notes.anchor: They were written and given out in such a form as to be displayed in public
notes.note: Pompey's replies to Caesar were drafted as public documents (proponi in publico) for posting and reading aloud, making them propaganda as much as diplomacy; this is why Cicero minds that the prose was left to the wooden stylist Sestius.

notes.anchor: if he had won the right earlier to be a candidate
notes.note: By the law of the ten tribunes (52 BC) Caesar had been granted the privilege of standing for a second consulship without entering Rome to declare his candidacy (in absentia); the Senate's later refusal to honour it was the proximate trigger of the war.

notes.anchor: I was on my own estates and had undertaken no levy and no business
notes.note: The dilectus was the conscription of citizens into the legions; Cicero stresses to Trebatius that, though he held imperium from his Cilician command, he had raised no troops and taken on no public charge, keeping himself studiously neutral while peace remained possible.
